decisions.title: Bootstrap writes a skeleton config; accept-all overwrites a skeleton but never-clobbers a populated config
decisions.context: FGAP-001: init / switch -c leave the substrate config-less, and the only config-originating op (accept-all) is whole-catalog all-or-nothing, so the custom-vocabulary path has no tool surface.
decisions.decision: 1) initProject and switchAndCreate write a minimal schema-valid skeleton config { schema_version: '1.0.0', block_kinds: [], root, substrate_id } after dir scaffolding, via a never-clobber writeSkeletonConfig (no-op when a config already exists). writeSkeletonConfig mints AND registers the substrate_id (registerSubstrate into the project-root registry), so identity exists and is registered from the first bootstrap action. 2) Skeleton-vs-populated is decided by content: isSkeletonConfig returns true only when EVERY addressable config registry is empty/absent. The registry set is derived by iterating REGISTRY_DESCRIPTORS (all 13 registries: block_kinds, relation_types, lenses, layers, invariants, hierarchy, installed_schemas, installed_blocks, tool_operations, tool_operations_forbidden, status_buckets, naming, display_strings), with a per-kind emptiness test (map -> no keys; array kinds -> zero length). Only schema_version / root / substrate_id are skeleton-permitted; content in ANY registry makes it non-skeleton. No config-schema change and no marker field. 3) adoptConception's guard is if(existing && !isSkeletonConfig(existing)) -- accept-all overwrites a skeleton but never-clobbers a populated config; over a skeleton it reads the existing on-disk substrate_id and preserves it (re-upserting the same registry entry), so init->accept-all keeps one stable, registered identity. 4) deriveBootstrapState gains a 'skeleton' state ordered no-pointer -> no-config -> skeleton -> not-installed -> ready; no-config is retained for a deleted-config substrate; a non-empty-block_kinds config with nothing to install is 'ready'. 5) A shared never-clobber writeSkeletonConfig(cwd, ctx?) writes the skeleton only when config.json is absent, via writeConfigForDir (AJV-validated atomic). 6) context-init / accept-all / switch-create op descriptions name the two onward paths: accept-all (adopt the catalog) or amend-config/edit (build custom).
consequences: accept-all.test.ts idempotent test reworked to skeleton semantics: first adopt on a skeleton => adopted:true (overwrite), second => adopted:false (now populated); add a populated-config never-clobber case.
accept-all.test.ts 'init scaffolds dirs only, no defaults' retargeted: init now writes config.json; assert exactly ['config.json'] present, no block data files.
context-sdk.test.ts keeps the genuine config-absent no-config test and adds a skeleton-state test.
The custom-vocabulary substrate path becomes fully tool-driven (skeleton -> amend-config add block_kinds -> write-schema -> append-block-item, no accept-all).
Substrate identity (substrate_id) is stable from the first bootstrap action rather than first appearing at accept-all.

decisions.title: Substrate clone is a fork: fresh substrate_id + substrate_derived_from_substrate lineage; registry becomes a provenance-bearing identity ledger
decisions.context: FGAP-002: no substrate-level import path and substrate identity without provenance. Surfaced by the switch-register gap — switching onto a foreign config cannot be distinguished from registry drift, and a blind register-on-switch cannot tell native-lost-registry from foreign-import.
decisions.decision: Clone uses FORK semantics, not mirror. cloneSubstrate copies the source substrate's content (config vocabulary + all blocks + relations.json + objects/) into a destination dir and MINTS A FRESH substrate_id in the destination, recording a substrate_derived_from_substrate edge to the source id — the substrate-level parallel to promoteItem's fresh-oid + item_derived_from_item. No substrate_id is duplicated across dirs, preserving the one-id<->one-dir SoT invariant. The project-root registry entry gains origin/provenance (minted-here vs derived-from <source id>, plus imported_at), turning it from a flat dir-map into an identity ledger. The SoT-drift invariant (context-sdk.ts:1685+) is refined to distinguish native-lost-registry / foreign-import / genuine drift rather than emitting one substrate_id_unregistered code. pi-context clone <source-substrate> <dest-dir> is the dual-surface command (library + Pi tool + CLI + orchestrator script). Mirror semantics (the same substrate_id live in two dirs) are rejected: they break one-id<->one-dir and the content-addressed uniqueness model.
consequences: Registry schema/type gains provenance fields (origin kind + source substrate_id + imported_at); registerSubstrate call sites updated to record origin.
New relation_type substrate_derived_from_substrate registered in config (substrate-level parallel to item_derived_from_item).
New cloneSubstrate library fn + Pi tool + CLI + orchestrator script, landed as one dual-surface unit.
The SoT-drift invariant is refined to provenance-aware states, eliminating the false substrate_id_unregistered on legitimate imports.
The local switch-register fix is constrained: switch auto-registers only a native (minted-here) substrate; a foreign unregistered id routes to clone/import instead of being silently absorbed.
The existing registry aliases field composes with provenance to name an imported substrate.

decisions.title: Switch reconciles a native identity: register config.substrate_id -> dir when absent; mismatch still errors
decisions.context: FGAP-003: neither switch path records the active substrate's identity, so a valid switch to an unregistered substrate false-fails context-validate with substrate_id_unregistered.
decisions.decision: switchToExisting and switchAndCreate register config.substrate_id -> active dir via registerSubstrate when the id is ABSENT from the project-root registry (idempotent). Register-if-absent is functionally safe: an absent id cannot collide, so registering it in this project's local namespace is harmless; a present-but-different-dir entry is the substrate_id_registry_mismatch case and remains an error, so genuine drift still surfaces. This decision records only the NATIVE re-registration; origin/provenance for imported (foreign) substrates is out of scope here and handled by the provenance ledger in DEC-0002 — a foreign substrate is brought in via clone, not silently absorbed by switch. DEC-0003 is the native-only case of DEC-0002's model.
consequences: switchToExisting + switchAndCreate gain a register-if-absent step after the pointer flip.
Eliminates the false substrate_id_unregistered on a valid switch.
Does NOT record origin/provenance — that enrichment lands with DEC-0002; until then a switched-in id is registered without lineage.
Tests: switch to an unregistered-but-present-config substrate -> registered + context-validate clean; switch where the id maps to a different dir -> substrate_id_registry_mismatch preserved.

decisions.title: Structure-aware git merge driver + post-merge integrity pass — pi-context plugs into git's 3-way merge so substrate files merge data-model-valid, not line-mangled
decisions.context: FGAP-004: substrate files fall through to git's line-based merge and get mangled. Git owns branches, commits, merge-base, refs, and invocation; pi-context supplies only the structure-aware per-file reconcile plus a whole-substrate post-merge pass. No native version DAG, snapshot roots, or branch-pinning — git provides all of that.
decisions.decision: 1) Register a git merge driver: .gitattributes maps substrate files (<block>.json, relations.json, config.json, schemas) to merge=pi-context, with a [merge "pi-context"] driver entry. Git computes the merge base and invokes the driver per file with %O (ancestor) / %A (ours) / %B (theirs) / %P (path). 2) Per-file structure-aware 3-way: parse the three versions; match items by stable id/oid (never array index) — added-on-one -> include; removed-on-one & untouched-on-other -> remove; modified-on-one -> take; modified-on-both -> field-level merge; removed-on-one & modified-on-other -> conflict. 3) Field-kind-dispatched field merge: scalar/object -> the side changed vs base (both-identical -> take; both-different -> declared deterministic policy or conflict); SEQUENCE fields -> the convergent ordered-sequence field-type (FGAP-005) run on the field's three versions. 4) Edges (relations.json) merge by 3-way set ops on (parent, child, relation_type) triples. 5) objects/: distinct content-hash-named files don't collide; git's directory merge unions them (equal hash == equal content) — no driver needed. 6) config/schema 3-way; divergent redefinition of the same block_kind / relation_type / schema -> surfaced conflict. 7) Post-merge pass (git post-merge hook OR a pi-context merge-finalize command — a per-file driver cannot see across files): cross-block referential integrity (drop or conflict edges whose endpoints were deleted in another file) + full declared-invariant + lens-validator revalidation (branch-valid states can merge to globally invalid). 8) Typed structured conflict set ({item_id, field, base, ours, theirs} + edge/schema analogues), surfaced by the post-merge pass, not inline text markers. 9) Branches, merge-base, commit lineage, refs, and cherry-pick remain git's — not reimplemented; the driver only makes git's per-file result data-model-valid.
consequences: No native version DAG, snapshot roots, branch refs, or merge-base finder — git supplies all of them. Branch-pinning is not needed and is not filed.

content_parent / content_hash keep their existing content-addressing + drift roles; merge does NOT use them for base-finding (git's %O is the base). Item-matching inside the driver uses the stable id/oid.
The merge driver + per-file merge logic + field-kind dispatch live in block-api; the cross-block-integrity + invariant revalidation pass is a git post-merge hook / merge-finalize command.
The convergent ordered-sequence field-type (FGAP-005) is the field-kind invoked inside the driver on %O/%A/%B — this is its host.
Scope is same-repo, shared-history branch merges; cross-project, no-shared-history import is clone (DEC-0002), an orthogonal axis git cannot merge.

decisions.title: Convergent ordered-sequence field-kind: non-interleaving Fugue-class CRDT over item-refs + move-CRDT position register, projected as a plain array
decisions.context: FGAP-005: ordered ref-collections need a sequence field that merges deterministically under concurrent insert/delete/move. This field-kind is one plugin into the substrate merge driver's field-merge dispatch (FGAP-004 / DEC-0004).
decisions.decision: 1) Authoritative representation: a non-interleaving sequence CRDT (Fugue-class) over item-refs; each element holds a unique, immutable position identifier minted relative to its neighbors at insert time; identifiers are densely orderable so an insert mints one identifier and mutates no neighbor; concurrent inserts into the same gap resolve to a deterministic order with no interleaving of distinct actors' runs. 2) Move as a first-class op via a position register (Kleppmann move-CRDT): an element's position is a register rewritten on move, resolved by hybrid logical clock; element identity is stable so a move never duplicates or drops the element, and two concurrent moves of the same element converge to one deterministic position. 3) Deterministic, causally-consistent resolution: the hybrid logical clock gives a replica-independent total order for same-gap insert ordering and move resolution; the same op-set in any arrival order yields the same sequence. 4) Field-aware merge driver: the substrate merge composes the CRDT op-sets / states for this field, at the field level. 5) Human-facing array projection: a sort-on-read helper emits the resolved sequence as a plain ordered ref-array for reading/editing; a hand-edit expressed as an array-position change is translated into CRDT ops through the block-api. Convergence lives in the stored representation; legibility lives in the projection. 6) Location: a declared sequence-field kind on the owning item; authoritative CRDT state carried in the owning <block>.json; the projection helper + field-aware merge driver live in the block-api.
consequences: A new declared field-kind (sequence-of-refs) recognized by schema + block-api, distinct from a plain ref-array.
CRDT state (position identifiers + position registers + HLC stamps) is stored in the owning <block>.json; the plain array becomes a read/edit projection, not the source of truth.
Plugs into the git merge driver (DEC-0004) field-merge dispatch as the sequence field-kind, invoked on git's %O/%A/%B of the owning block file; the driver is the host, this is the field-kind plugin.
Single-writer authoring order is reproduced identically to a plain array via sort-on-read, so existing read/edit ergonomics are preserved.
Latent until the structure-aware git merge driver (FGAP-004) exists; until then it degrades to the current single-writer array behavior.

decisions.title: Make the op-registry a faithful, attested, complete projection of the library: ctx through the op contract + full write-surface coverage + a parity test
decisions.context: FGAP-008/009/010 (and FGAP-006 as an instance): the op run(cwd, params) contract carries no DispatchContext, so cli/in-pi writes are attestation-blind (alpha); the registry omits library write capabilities (beta); nothing enforces parity across the library/op/script surfaces (gamma). The class is documented in analysis/2026-06-04-cli-op-registry-lossy-projection.md.
decisions.decision: (alpha) Add a writer/DispatchContext channel to the op-execution contract: OpDefinition.run gains a ctx/writer parameter, registerAll threads the pi ExtensionContext's verified identity, and the cli builds a DispatchContext from --writer for every write op — so attestation stamps uniformly rather than via per-op schema smuggling; context-archive's declared-but-ignored writer is fixed in passing. (beta) Complete the op surface against the library write set as dual-surface units (library + op + CLI + orchestrator script): add the missing removeRelation library primitive + a remove-relation / replace-relation op (closing FGAP-006), and ops for writeRelations / upsertItemInBlock / writeConfig / writeSchema+updateSchema / registerSubstrate+writeRegistry / writeBootstrapPointer+flipBootstrapPointer / bulk appendRelations as warranted. (gamma) Add a parity/coverage test that fails when a library write fn has no op or an op drops the ctx / optional params (dryRun, ordinal) its library fn or orchestrator-script sibling forwards; wire into husky + CI. The unified aim: the cli is a faithful, attested interface to everything one can do with pi-context out-of-pi.
consequences: The OpDefinition contract change ripples to all op handlers + registerAll + the cli dispatch + pi-workflows step-block (which must also thread ctx).
A new library primitive removeRelation + a batch of new ops; the op count grows toward library-write-surface parity.
The parity test becomes a standing guard: a new library writer without an op, or a ctx-dropping handler, fails the build.
FGAP-006 closes as part of beta; the append-block-item attestation instance closes as part of alpha.
Until landed, filing continues via the orchestrator scripts (which attest), not the cli.

decisions.title: Release & changelog discipline: changelog entries accrue at dev-item closure; a release is a planned decision grouping closed dev-items
decisions.context: FGAP-011: no first-class release/version vocabulary, so release + changelog tracking risks drifting from the organized dev-item lifecycle (closed tasks/gaps) — the 'we haven't kept track of changelogs/releases vs done items' failure. Until the vocabulary lands, this decision fixes the manual practice so the thread is not lost. It is the interim discipline; FGAP-011's release vocabulary is the eventual structural fix.
decisions.decision: 1) A changelog [Unreleased] entry is authored in the SAME code commit that completes a dev-item's published-surface work — not deferred to release time. The commit-time changelog guard already enforces that an entry exists; this binds the entry's CONTENT to the closing task/gap. 2) A release is an explicit DECISION: when we decide to release, we record it (a release decision, or — once FGAP-011 lands — a first-class release item) naming the target version and the set of closed dev-items it ships, grouped via a phase or story (task_positioned_in_phase / story_contains_task). 3) The release's changelog is the accrued [Unreleased] entries of those closed items; the version bump (release.mjs) stamps [Unreleased] -> [version]. So a release reflects the organized set of done dev-items, traceable item -> changelog line -> version. 4) This binding is practice-enforced until FGAP-011 provides a release block kind + a dev-item<->release relation that makes it structural.
consequences: Each task/gap closure that changes a package's published surface carries its changelog line in the closing code commit, not a batch write at release time.
A release is planned by an explicit decision (version + grouped closed items), not assembled ad-hoc.
The risk of changelog/release drift from the dev-item lifecycle is mitigated by practice now and is slated to become structural via FGAP-011.
Substrate status-cascade commits (.context) do not trigger the changelog guard; the discipline applies to the published-surface code commits that close items.
